rulings.Case Title: SIMMONS FIRST NATIONAL BANK VS. DAVID R STEWART et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 22, 2019, Line 1, DEFENDANT DAVID STEWART's MOTION FOR SET ASIDE/VACATE JUDGMENT Defendant David Stewart's motion to vacate the renewed judgment entered against him on August 2, 2016 is granted. Defendant has met his burden to show that he is entitled to relief under Code of Civil Procedure section 683.170 on the ground that he was never properly served with the summons and complaint in the underlying action. Fidelity Creditor Service, Inc. v. Browne (2001) 89 Cal.App.4th 195, 200-203. Plaintiff Simmons First National Bank has not shown that it could not with reasonable diligence have personally delivered a copy of the summons and complaint to defendant such that substitute service on defendant at a private or commercial post office box would have been proper pursuant to Code of Civil Procedure section 415.20(b). The process server's declaration of diligence reflects that he attempted to serve defendant only at the location of the private mailbox facility. Contrary to plaintiff's argument, whether the defendant had a meritorious defense to the underlying action is not a relevant consideration. Fidelity Creditor Service, Inc., 89 Cal.App.4th at 204-206. To the extent that defendant's motion seeks to recall and quash unspecified writs of execution or abstracts of judgment and to order the return of any property levied pursuant to the renewed judgment, it is denied without prejudice. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA VS. PROPERTY SUBJECT TO DISPOSITION: $129,000.00 U.S. et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers, To Petitioners First Demand For Document Production And To Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories; (Or In The Alternative Motion To Compel Further Answers To Demand For Document Production)
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, JANUARY 22, 2019, LINE 1 PETITIONER PEOPLE OF THE STATE OF CALIFORNIA Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Answers, To Petitioners First Demand For Document Production And To Petitioners First Set Of Special Interrogatories; (Or In The Alternative Motion To Compel Further Answers To Demand For Document Production) Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The parties shall address the circumstances surrounding service on August 25, 2014 of Claimant's response to Petitioner's Response to Request for Production of Documents. The Court notes absence of a declaration from Claimant's former counsel supporting completed service of a verified response as well as absence of a proof of service of that pleading. If the Court determines the 2014 served response was not verified, Claimant's Motion for Waiver of Deficiency is denied. Not provided is the required showing the deficiency failure was a result of mistake, inadvertence or excusable neglect. See C.C.P. 2030.290 (a)(2). Following resolution of those issues the Court will hear argument and rule on the objections to the recently re-served response to the subject discovery pleadings. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to sbs@sbslawsf.com with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. If the tentative ruling is not contested, the parties are deemed to have stipulated to the Pro Tem hearing the motion and the Pro Tem will sign an order confirming the tentative ruling. The prevailing party is required to prepare a proposed order repeating verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring the proposed order to the hearing even if the motion is not opposed or the tentative ruling is not contested. = SBS

rulings.Case Title: ***TRANSFERRED TO FRESNO SUPERIOR COURT*** SONA VARTANIAN et al VS. MCCORMICK BARSTOW SHEPPARD WAYTE & CARRUTH, et al
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO TRANSFER
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 22, 2019, Line 3, DEFENDANTs MCCORMICK BARSTOW SHEPPARD WAYTE & CARRUTH,, CHRISTOPHER HALL, and KATHLEEN HIPPS' MOTION TO TRANSFER Defendants' motion to transfer venue to the Superior Court of California, County of Fresno is granted as unopposed. The Court awards defendants $912 in reasonable expenses and attorney's fees incurring in making the motion to transfer; no fees are awarded for reviewing the late-filed opposition or for filing the unnecessary reply. In exercising its discretion under Code of Civil Procedure section 396b(b), the Court finds that the selection of venue was not made in good faith, given the facts and law the plaintiffs knew or should have known, which established that the County of San Francisco was not the proper venue for this action. Further, defendants first requested that plaintiffs stipulate to a change of venue on September 18, 2018, but plaintiffs did not accept that request before December 17, 2018, when the motion was filed. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: HARITH ALBADRI VS. ERIC SMIT ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination, Deposition, And Request For Sanctions
rulings.Rulings: SET FOR HEARING ON TUESDAY, JANUARY 22, 2019, LINE 2 DEFENDANT DEAN CRIDDLE Notice Of Motion And Motion To Compel Independent Medical Examination, Deposition, And Request For Sanctions Pro Tem Judge Steven Stein, a member of the California State Bar who meets all the requirements set forth in CRC 2.812 to serve as a temporary judge, has been assigned to hear this motion. Prior to the hearing all parties to the motion will be asked to sign a stipulation agreeing that the motion may be heard by the Pro Tem Judge. If all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the hearing will proceed before the Judge Pro Tem who will decide the motion with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. If a party appears by telephone, the stipulation may be signed via fax or consent to sign given by email. If not all parties to the motion sign the stipulation, the Pro Tem Judge will hold a hearing on the motion and, based on the papers submitted by the parties and the hearing, issue a report in the nature of a recommendation to the Dept. 302 Judge, who will then decide the motion. If a party does not appear at the hearing, the party will be deemed to have stipulated that the motion will be decided by the Pro Tem Judge with the same authority as a Superior Court Judge. The Pro Tem Judge has issued the following tentative ruling: Hearing required. The Court intends to Order a physical examination of the plaintiff with specific examination terms and an oral deposition of plaintiff, both on dates to be agreed by the parties. The scope of the examination will be limited to those body parts for which a claim of injury is asserted. Examination of the plaintiff's left shoulder to compare with the injured right shoulder is not unreasonable. The Court will hear argument regarding the need for further non-injured body-part examination and rule whether examination is necessary. The defendant shall cover all costs incurred by plaintiff for travel and lodging to and within the Bay Area. Requests for sanctions will be denied. rulings.Case Title: DANIEL O'CONNELL VS. DHARMESH PATEL ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Notice And Motion Of Defendants Dharmesh Patel And Sixteenth Street Hotel Llc For Summary Judgment
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 22, 2019, Line 5, DEFENDANTs DHARMESH PATEL and SIXTEENTH STREET HOTEL, LLC (ERRONEOUSLY SUED AS 16TH STREET HOTEL, LLC)'S MOTION FOR SUMMARY JUDGMENT Defendants Dharmesh Patel, 16th Street Hotel LLC, amd Sixteenth Street Hotel, LLC's motion for summary judgment is denied. Plaintiff has identified facts-the absence of a second handrail and defendants' failure to provide adequate illumination of the stairway-from which a reasonable jury could infer that a dangerous condition existed and was a substantial factor in bringing about Mr. O'Connell's injuries. In Buehler v. Alpha Beta Co. (1990) 224 Cal.App.3d 729, in contrast, defendant met its burden of proof on summary judgment to show the absence of a dangerous condition. Harpke v. Lankershim Estates (1951) 103 Cal.App.2d 143, 145, which held that no inference of negligence arises from the mere proof of a fall upon a stairway, is distinguishable on that ground and because it did not involve summary judgment. Triable issues of material fact also exist as as to whether defendants' alleged violations of San Francisco Building Code §§ 504 (g) and 802(c) were the proximate cause of plaintiff's injuries under a negligence per se theory. (O'Connell Depo. pp. 90, 92, 94; O'Connell verified responses to interrogatories, Jessup Decl. Exh. 4, Set One (1), Nos. 15, 21). Causation in fact ordinarily presents a question of fact which cannot be resolved on summary judgment. Vasquez v. Residential Investments, Inc. (2004) 118 Cal.App.4th 269, 288-289. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for plaintiff is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)

rulings.Case Title: AMARI CHAMBERS VS. PAUL CHEN ET AL
rulings.Calendar Matter: MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT
rulings.Rulings: Matter on calendar for Tuesday, January 22, 2019, Line 9, DEFENDANT PAUL CHEN'S MOTION TO STRIKE COMPLAINT Defendant Paul Chen's motion to strike those portions of the complaint that seek punitive damages is granted. See Turman v. Turning Point of Central California, Inc. (2010) 191 Cal.App.4th 53, 63 (affirming order striking punitive damages allegations). In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud or malice. Civ. Code § 3294(a). "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." Civ. Code § 3294(c)(1). Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725. Punitive damages are appropriate if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent or in blatant violation of law or policy. The mere carelessness or ignorance of the defendant does not justify the imposition of punitive damages. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-1176. The allegations in the complaint that defendant drove a motor vehicle while illegally texting or otherwise impermissibly using a cellphone or similar electronic device, in and of themselves, do not rise to the level of malice necessary under Civil Code section 3294 to state a claim for punitive damages. See Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210 (evidence that snowboarder who struck skier was not "despicable" so as to support punitive damages claim; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her). Taylor v. Superior Court (1979) 24 Cal.3d 890, relied upon by plaintiff, is distinguishable both because it was decided before the statute was amended in 1987 to require a showing of despicable conduct and on its facts. Id. at 1212 & n.14. Any party who contests a tentative ruling must send an email to contestdept302tr@sftc.org with a copy to all other parties by 4pm stating, without argument, the portion(s) of the tentative ruling that the party contests. The subject line of the email shall include the line number, case name and case number. Counsel for the plaintiffs is required to prepare a proposed order which repeats verbatim the substantive portion of the tentative ruling and must bring it to the hearing or email it to contestdept302tr@sftc.org prior to the hearing even if the tentative ruling is not contested. =(302/EPS)
